import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
data_path="data/question_5_data/train.csv"
df = pd.read_csv(data_path)
print (f"Shape: {df.shape}")
print (f"Data types:\n{df.dtypes}")
print ("\n ================= First 5 rows =================\n")
print(df.head())

#ii)
missing_counts = df.isnull().sum()
missing_percentages = (df.isnull().sum() / len(df)) * 100

#Create DataFrame
missing_df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Missing Values': missing_counts,
    'Percentage (%)': missing_percentages
}).sort_values(by='Missing Values', ascending=False)


print("--- Missing Data Summary ---")
print(missing_df)

# 4. Visualize the results
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
missing_df['Percentage (%)'].plot(kind='bar', color='skyblue')
plt.title('Percentage of Missing Values per Column')
plt.ylabel('Percentage (%)')
plt.xlabel('Columns')
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.grid(axis='y', linestyle='--', alpha=0.7)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

In [223]:
# iv)
numerical_features = df.select_dtypes(include=['float64', 'int64']).columns.tolist()

# Determine grid size for subplots
n_features = len(numerical_features)
ncols = 3
nrows = (n_features + ncols - 1) // ncols

fig, axes = plt.subplots(nrows, ncols, figsize=(16, 4 * nrows))
axes = axes.flatten()

for i, feature in enumerate(numerical_features):
    # Dropping NaN values for plotting
    axes[i].hist(df[feature].dropna(), bins=30, color='steelblue', edgecolor='black')
    axes[i].set_title(feature, fontsize=12)
    axes[i].set_xlabel('Value')
    axes[i].set_ylabel('Frequency')

# Hide any unused subplots
for j in range(i + 1, len(axes)):
    axes[j].axis('off')

plt.suptitle('Histograms of All Numerical Input Features', fontsize=16, fontweight='bold', y=1.02)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

All the features except for age are very heavily right skewed, which means most passengers did not have access to those in the spaceship

In [224]:
# v)
import seaborn as sns

categorical_features = ['HomePlanet', 'Destination', 'CryoSleep', 'VIP']

# Set up the matplotlib figure
fig, axes = plt.subplots(nrows=4, ncols=2, figsize=(12, 16))
fig.tight_layout(pad=5.0)

for i, col in enumerate(categorical_features):
    
    sns.countplot(data=df, x=col, ax=axes[i, 0])
    axes[i, 0].set_title(f'Count of {col}', fontsize=12)
    axes[i, 0].set_ylabel('Count')
    
    sns.barplot(data=df, x=col, y='Transported', ax=axes[i, 1])
    axes[i, 1].set_title(f'Transportation Rate by {col}', fontsize=12)
    axes[i, 1].set_ylabel('Fraction Transported')
    # Optional: Add a horizontal line representing the baseline transportation rate
    baseline_rate = df['Transported'].mean()
    axes[i, 1].axhline(baseline_rate, color='red', linestyle='--', alpha=0.6, label='Overall Mean')
    axes[i, 1].legend()

plt.show()

The Cryo Sleep category appears to be the most predictive of the target since around 80% of passengers in Cryo Sleep were transported

In [225]:
# vi)

import numpy as np
spending_features = ['RoomService', 'FoodCourt', 'ShoppingMall', 'Spa', 'VRDeck']

# Set up the matplotlib figure
fig, axes = plt.subplots(nrows=2, ncols=3, figsize=(14, 16))
axes = axes.flatten()

for i, col in enumerate(spending_features):
    # Using a violin plot. Box plot can be used by swapping sns.violinplot with sns.boxplot
    # We apply a log transformation (log1p) because spending data is highly right-skewed 
    # with many passengers spending 0. This makes the plots much easier to read.
    sns.violinplot(
        data=df, 
        x='Transported', 
        y=df[col], # log(1 + x) to handle 0s
        ax=axes[i], 
    )
    axes[i].set_title(f'{col} Spending by Transported', fontsize=12)
    axes[i].set_ylabel(f'{col}')

# Remove the empty 6th subplot
fig.delaxes(axes[5])

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Transported passengers tend to spend significantly less, which reinforces the hypothesis that being in cryo sleep is one of the most important factors to predict the target (since sleeping passengers do not spend)

Passenger ID gives us no information about the passengers: it is simply a unique identifier (same logic applies to name, except it is not unique). Cabin could be useful, but it is too complex to be processed in the scope of this problem.

# We can just convert the booleans to ints
df['CryoSleep'] = df['CryoSleep'].astype(int)
df['VIP'] = df['VIP'].astype(int)

df = pd.get_dummies(df, columns=['HomePlanet', 'Destination'], drop_first=True, dtype=int)

num_features = df.shape[1] - 1 if 'Transported' in df.columns else df.shape[1]

# v)
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
import numpy as np

np.random.seed(42)

X = df.drop(columns=['Transported'])
y = df['Transported'] #target

# Separate out the Test set (20%) from the Train+Val set (80%)
X_train_val, X_test, y_train_val, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, y, 
    test_size=0.20, 
    random_state=42, 
    stratify=y  # Ensures class balance is maintained
)

# Separate the Train+Val set (80%) into Train (60%) and Val (20%)
X_train, X_val, y_train, y_val = train_test_split(
    X_train_val, y_train_val, 
    test_size=0.25, 
    random_state=42, 
    stratify=y_train_val # Ensures class balance is maintained
)
X_train_raw = X_train.copy() # Keep a copy of the raw training features for later use in part (e)
X_test_raw = X_test.copy()   # Keep a copy of the raw test features for later use in part (e)
X_val_raw = X_val.copy()     # Keep a copy of the raw validation features for later use in part (e)

# Verify
print(f"Total samples:  {len(df)}")
print("-" * 30)
print(f"Training set:   {len(X_train)} samples ({len(X_train)/len(df)*100:.1f}%)")
print(f"Validation set: {len(X_val)} samples ({len(X_val)/len(df)*100:.1f}%)")
print(f"Test set:       {len(X_test)} samples ({len(X_test)/len(df)*100:.1f}%)")

------------------------------
Training set:   5215 samples (60.0%)
Validation set: 1739 samples (20.0%)
Test set:       1739 samples (20.0%)

# vi)
for feature in X_train.columns:
    mean = X_train[feature].mean()
    std = X_train[feature].std()
    X_train[feature] = (X_train[feature] - mean) / std
    X_val[feature] = (X_val[feature] - mean) / std
    X_test[feature] = (X_test[feature] - mean) / std
In [ ]:
#Part c)
# i) Part i is done by hand in the first page of the submission.
# ii)
#helper functions:
def sigmoid(z):
    z = np.clip(z, -250, 250) 
    return 1 / (1 + np.exp(-z))

def compute_ce_loss(X, y, w):
    N = len(y)
    y_hat = sigmoid(np.dot(X, w))
    
    # Add a tiny value (epsilon) to prevent taking log(0)
    epsilon = 1e-15 
    loss = - (1/N) * np.sum(y * np.log(y_hat + epsilon) + (1 - y) * np.log(1 - y_hat + epsilon))
    return loss

def gradient_descent_ce(X, y, learning_rate=0.1, num_iterations=1000):
    N, num_features = X.shape
    
    # Initialize weights w to a vector of zeros
    w = np.zeros(num_features)
    
    # Store the loss at each step to plot convergence later
    loss_history = []
    
    for t in range(num_iterations):
        # Predicted probabilities: sigma(w^T * X)
        y_hat = sigmoid(np.dot(X, w))
        
        # Gradient: (1/N) * X^T * (y_hat - y)
        gradient = (1/N) * np.dot(X.T, (y_hat - y))
        
        # w(t+1)
        w = w - learning_rate * gradient
        
        # Calculate loss every 100 iterations or at the very last
        if t % 100 == 0 or t == num_iterations - 1:
            loss = compute_ce_loss(X, y, w)
            loss_history.append((t, loss))
            
    return w, loss_history

w, loss_history = gradient_descent_ce(X_train.values, y_train.values, learning_rate=0.1, num_iterations=1000)

# Unpack the list of tuples into two separate lists
iterations, losses = zip(*loss_history)

# Plot the unpacked lists
plt.plot(iterations, losses, marker='o')
plt.title('CE loss over iterations')
plt.xlabel('Iteration')
plt.ylabel('CE loss')
plt.show()

val_probs = sigmoid(np.dot(X_val.values, w))

train_preds = (train_probs >= 0.5).astype(int)
val_preds = (val_probs >= 0.5).astype(int)

train_accuracy = np.mean(train_preds == y_train.values)
val_accuracy = np.mean(val_preds == y_val.values)

print(f"Training Accuracy:   {train_accuracy * 100:.2f}%")
print(f"Validation Accuracy: {val_accuracy * 100:.2f}%")

In [234]:
# Part d)
# i) Part i is done by hand in the first page of the submission.
# ii)
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt


def compute_mse_loss(X, y, w):
    N = len(y)
    y_hat = sigmoid(np.dot(X, w))
    loss = (1/N) * np.sum((y_hat - y)**2)
    return loss

#Same as ce, with different loss function
def gradient_descent_mse(X, y, learning_rate=0.1, num_iterations=1000):
    N, num_features = X.shape
    w = np.zeros(num_features)
    loss_history = []
    
    for t in range(num_iterations):
        y_hat = sigmoid(np.dot(X, w))
        
        gradient = (2/N) * np.dot(X.T, (y_hat - y) * y_hat * (1 - y_hat))
        
        w = w - learning_rate * gradient
        
        if t % 100 == 0 or t == num_iterations - 1:
            loss = compute_mse_loss(X, y, w)
            loss_history.append((t, loss))
            
    return w, loss_history

w_mse, mse_loss_history = gradient_descent_mse(
    X_train.values, y_train.values, 
    learning_rate=0.1, 
    num_iterations=1000
)

# Unpack both histories
ce_iterations, ce_losses = zip(*loss_history) # from (c)(ii)
mse_iterations, mse_losses = zip(*mse_loss_history)

# 3. Plotting with dual y-axes
fig, ax1 = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))

# Plot Cross-Entropy on the primary (left) y-axis
color_ce = 'tab:blue'
ax1.set_xlabel('Iteration')
ax1.set_ylabel('Cross-Entropy Loss', color=color_ce)
ax1.plot(ce_iterations, ce_losses, color=color_ce, marker='o', label='CE Loss')
ax1.tick_params(axis='y', labelcolor=color_ce)

# MSE on the secondary (right) y-axis
ax2 = ax1.twinx()  
color_mse = 'tab:red'
ax2.set_ylabel('MSE Loss', color=color_mse)  
ax2.plot(mse_iterations, mse_losses, color=color_mse, marker='s', linestyle='--', label='MSE Loss')
ax2.tick_params(axis='y', labelcolor=color_mse)

# Title and legends
plt.title('Training Loss Curves: CE vs. MSE')
fig.tight_layout() 
fig.legend(loc='center right', bbox_to_anchor=(0.85, 0.5))

plt.show()

val_preds = (val_probs >= 0.5).astype(int)

train_accuracy = np.mean(train_preds == y_train.values)

print(f"Validation Accuracy for CE: {val_accuracy * 100:.2f}%")

val_probs_mse = sigmoid(np.dot(X_val.values, w_mse))

train_preds_mse = (train_probs_mse >= 0.5).astype(int)
val_preds_mse = (val_probs_mse >= 0.5).astype(int)

train_accuracy_mse = np.mean(train_preds_mse == y_train.values)
val_accuracy_mse = np.mean(val_preds_mse == y_val.values)

print(f"MSE Training Accuracy for MSE:   {train_accuracy_mse * 100:.2f}%")
print(f"MSE Validation Accuracy for MSE: {val_accuracy_mse * 100:.2f}%")

Training Accuracy for CE:   78.83%
Validation Accuracy for CE: 77.00%
MSE Training Accuracy for MSE:   78.95%
MSE Validation Accuracy for MSE: 77.11%

The MSE performance is slightly better than CE, although similar. The slightly edge of MSE is expected because of the sigmoid derivative term which makes it very hard to correct very confident labels. The difference is not as noticible in this problems because weights were initialize at one of the extremes (all zeros)

# Part e)
# i)

spending_cols = ['RoomService', 'FoodCourt', 'ShoppingMall', 'Spa', 'VRDeck']

raw_spend_train = X_train_raw[spending_cols].sum(axis=1)
raw_spend_val = X_val_raw[spending_cols].sum(axis=1)

X_train['NoSpending'] = (raw_spend_train == 0).astype(int)
X_val['NoSpending'] = (raw_spend_val == 0).astype(int)

# log1p is log (1 + x)
log_spend_train = np.log1p(raw_spend_train)
log_spend_val = np.log1p(raw_spend_val)

#standardize
log_spend_mean = log_spend_train.mean()
log_spend_std = log_spend_train.std()
X_train['LogTotalSpending'] = (log_spend_train - log_spend_mean) / log_spend_std
X_val['LogTotalSpending'] = (log_spend_val - log_spend_mean) / log_spend_std


# Retrain the CE model
w_eng, loss_history_eng = gradient_descent_ce(
    X_train.values, y_train.values, 
    learning_rate=0.1, 
    num_iterations=1000
)

train_probs_eng = sigmoid(np.dot(X_train.values, w_eng))
val_probs_eng = sigmoid(np.dot(X_val.values, w_eng))

train_preds_eng = (train_probs_eng >= 0.5).astype(int)
val_preds_eng = (val_probs_eng >= 0.5).astype(int)

train_acc_eng = np.mean(train_preds_eng == y_train.values)
val_acc_eng = np.mean(val_preds_eng == y_val.values)

print(f"New features Training Accuracy:   {train_acc_eng * 100:.2f}%")
print(f"New features Validation Accuracy: {val_acc_eng * 100:.2f}%")

New features Training Accuracy:   79.04%
New features Validation Accuracy: 77.57%

The new features help increasing the validation accuracy by 0.57%

In [ ]:
# ii)
from itertools import combinations_with_replacement

spending_cols = ['RoomService', 'FoodCourt', 'ShoppingMall', 'Spa', 'VRDeck']
interaction_features = []

# Construct degree-2 interactions for j <= k
for col1, col2 in combinations_with_replacement(spending_cols, 2):
    new_col_name = f"{col1}_x_{col2}"
    interaction_features.append(new_col_name)
    
    # Multiply the columns together
    X_train[new_col_name] = X_train[col1] * X_train[col2]
    X_val[new_col_name] = X_val[col1] * X_val[col2]
    
    # Standardize the new interaction feature using ONLY training set statistics
    mean_val = X_train[new_col_name].mean()
    std_val = X_train[new_col_name].std()
    
    # Avoid division by zero in case a feature is perfectly uniform
    if std_val > 0: 
        X_train[new_col_name] = (X_train[new_col_name] - mean_val) / std_val
        X_val[new_col_name] = (X_val[new_col_name] - mean_val) / std_val

print(f"Added {len(interaction_features)} new polynomial features.\n")

# Retrain 
w_poly, loss_history_poly = gradient_descent_ce(
    X_train.values, y_train.values, 
    learning_rate=0.1, 
    num_iterations=1000
)

# Evaluate model
train_probs_poly = sigmoid(np.dot(X_train.values, w_poly))
val_probs_poly = sigmoid(np.dot(X_val.values, w_poly))

train_preds_poly = (train_probs_poly >= 0.5).astype(int)
val_preds_poly = (val_probs_poly >= 0.5).astype(int)

train_acc_poly = np.mean(train_preds_poly == y_train.values)
val_acc_poly = np.mean(val_preds_poly == y_val.values)

print(f"Polynomial Training Accuracy:   {train_acc_poly * 100:.2f}%")
print(f"Polynomial Validation Accuracy: {val_acc_poly * 100:.2f}%")

Added 15 new polynomial features.

Polynomial Training Accuracy:   79.33%
Polynomial Validation Accuracy: 77.52%

iii) The polynomial expansion slightly decreases accuracy by 0.05%, which indicates that the decision boundary is most likely linear.

In [ ]:
# Part f
# i) done by hand in the first page of the submission
# ii)
def gradient_descent_ce_lambda(X, y, l, learning_rate=0.1, num_iterations=1000):
    N, num_features = X.shape
    
    # Initialize weights w to a vector of zeros
    w = np.zeros(num_features)
    
    # Store the loss at each step to plot convergence later
    loss_history = []
    
    for t in range(num_iterations):
        # Predicted probabilities: sigma(w^T * X)
        y_hat = sigmoid(np.dot(X, w))
        
        # Gradient: (1/N) * X^T * (y_hat - y) + lambda * w
        # Notice the + sign and the use of the w vector directly!
        gradient = (1/N) * np.dot(X.T, (y_hat - y)) + l * w
        
        # w(t+1)
        w = w - learning_rate * gradient
        
        # Calculate regularized loss every 100 iterations or at the very last
        if t % 100 == 0 or t == num_iterations - 1:
            base_ce_loss = compute_ce_loss(X, y, w)
            # Add the L2 penalty term to the loss history
            reg_loss = base_ce_loss + (l / 2) * np.sum(w**2) 
            loss_history.append((t, reg_loss))
            
    return w, loss_history

lambdas = [0, 1e-4, 1e-3, 1e-2, 1e-1, 1]
train_acc_list = []
val_acc_list = []

for l in lambdas:
    w, loss_history = gradient_descent_ce_lambda(X_train.values, y_train.values, l, learning_rate=0.1, num_iterations=1000)
    
    train_probs_lambda = sigmoid(np.dot(X_train.values, w))
    val_probs_lambda = sigmoid(np.dot(X_val.values, w))

    train_preds_lambda = (train_probs_lambda >= 0.5).astype(int)
    val_preds_lambda = (val_probs_lambda >= 0.5).astype(int)

    train_acc_lambda = np.mean(train_preds_lambda == y_train.values)
    val_acc_lambda = np.mean(val_preds_lambda == y_val.values)

    print(f"λ={l:<6} | Train Acc: {train_acc_lambda * 100:.2f}% | Val Acc: {val_acc_lambda * 100:.2f}%")
    
    train_acc_list.append(train_acc_lambda)
    val_acc_list.append(val_acc_lambda)


plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))

x_positions = np.arange(len(lambdas)) 
# Convert the lambda values to clean strings for the labels
x_labels = [str(l) for l in lambdas]  

plt.plot(x_positions, train_acc_list, marker='o', color='tab:blue', linewidth=2, label='Training Accuracy')
plt.plot(x_positions, val_acc_list, marker='s', color='tab:orange', linewidth=2, linestyle='--', label='Validation Accuracy')

# Apply the custom string labels to the evenly spaced x-axis
plt.xticks(x_positions, x_labels)

plt.xlabel('Lambda (L2 Penalty)')
plt.ylabel('Accuracy')
plt.title('Effect of L2 Regularization on Model Accuracy')
plt.legend()
plt.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.6)
plt.tight_layout()

plt.show()

λ=0      | Train Acc: 79.33% | Val Acc: 77.52%
λ=0.0001 | Train Acc: 79.35% | Val Acc: 77.52%
λ=0.001  | Train Acc: 79.19% | Val Acc: 77.34%
λ=0.01   | Train Acc: 78.49% | Val Acc: 77.11%
λ=0.1    | Train Acc: 77.79% | Val Acc: 76.54%
λ=1      | Train Acc: 75.76% | Val Acc: 74.35%

The best hyperparameter is surprisingly 0. Regularization works as a way of fighting overfitting in non-linear polynomial functions as the term lambda * weights drags down the weights towards 0, which avoids the high weights that cause overfitting.

In [239]:
#Part g)
# i)
from sklearn.model_selection import KFold

kf = KFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=42)

cv_accuracies = []

for l in lambdas:
    fold_accuracies = []
    
    #Iterate through the 5 distinct folds
    for train_idx, val_idx in kf.split(X_train.values):
        
        # Split the current training data into CV-train and CV-validation subsets
        X_cv_train = X_train.values[train_idx]
        X_cv_val = X_train.values[val_idx]
        y_cv_train = y_train.values[train_idx]
        y_cv_val = y_train.values[val_idx]
        
        # Train the model on the 4 CV-train folds
        w, _ = gradient_descent_ce_lambda(
            X_cv_train, y_cv_train, l=l, 
            learning_rate=0.1, num_iterations=1000
        )
        
        # Evaluate on the last CV-validation fold
        val_probs = sigmoid(np.dot(X_cv_val, w))
        val_preds = (val_probs >= 0.5).astype(int)
        
        fold_acc = np.mean(val_preds == y_cv_val)
        fold_accuracies.append(fold_acc)
        
    #Average the validation performance over all 5 folds
    avg_acc = np.mean(fold_accuracies)
    cv_accuracies.append(avg_acc)
    
    print(f"λ = {l:<6} | Average CV Validation Accuracy: {avg_acc * 100:.2f}%")

print("-" * 55)
best_idx = np.argmax(cv_accuracies)
best_lambda = lambdas[best_idx]
best_acc = cv_accuracies[best_idx]

print(f"Best Regularization Parameter (λ): {best_lambda}")
print(f"Highest Average CV Accuracy:       {best_acc * 100:.2f}%")

λ = 0      | Average CV Validation Accuracy: 78.96%
λ = 0.0001 | Average CV Validation Accuracy: 78.96%
λ = 0.001  | Average CV Validation Accuracy: 79.06%
λ = 0.01   | Average CV Validation Accuracy: 78.56%
λ = 0.1    | Average CV Validation Accuracy: 77.58%
λ = 1      | Average CV Validation Accuracy: 75.59%
-------------------------------------------------------
Best Regularization Parameter (λ): 0.001
Highest Average CV Accuracy:       79.06%

ii) The best parameter changed from 0 to 0.001 using the 5-k fold method. One possible explanation is that the validation set for the regular method favored 0, while k-fold uses different folds to find the best performance.

In [240]:
from itertools import combinations_with_replacement

# We assume X_test here is your PROPERLY PREPROCESSED test set (scaled, encoded, etc.)
spending_cols = ['RoomService', 'FoodCourt', 'ShoppingMall', 'Spa', 'VRDeck']

# Calculate the math using X_test_raw, but append the results to X_test
raw_spend_test = X_test_raw[spending_cols].sum(axis=1)
X_test['NoSpending'] = (raw_spend_test == 0).astype(int)

raw_spend_train = X_train_raw[spending_cols].sum(axis=1)
log_spend_train = np.log1p(raw_spend_train)
X_test['LogTotalSpending'] = (np.log1p(raw_spend_test) - log_spend_train.mean()) / log_spend_train.std()

# add features to x_test
for col1, col2 in combinations_with_replacement(spending_cols, 2):
    new_col_name = f"{col1}_x_{col2}"
    
    # Calculate the raw interaction using X_test_raw
    raw_test_interaction = X_test_raw[col1] * X_test_raw[col2]
    
    # Calculate training stats for scaling
    raw_train_interaction = X_train_raw[col1] * X_train_raw[col2]
    train_mean = raw_train_interaction.mean()
    train_std = raw_train_interaction.std()
    
    # Standardize and append to the preprocessed X_test
    if train_std > 0:
        X_test[new_col_name] = (raw_test_interaction - train_mean) / train_std
    else:
        X_test[new_col_name] = 0

# kfold
w_cv, _ = gradient_descent_ce_lambda(X_train.values, y_train.values, l=0.001, learning_rate=0.1, num_iterations=1000)
test_probs_cv = sigmoid(np.dot(X_test.values, w_cv))
test_preds_cv = (test_probs_cv >= 0.5).astype(int)
test_acc_cv = np.mean(test_preds_cv == y_test.values)

# single split
w_single, _ = gradient_descent_ce_lambda(X_train.values, y_train.values, l=0, learning_rate=0.1, num_iterations=1000)
test_probs_single = sigmoid(np.dot(X_test.values, w_single))
test_preds_single = (test_probs_single >= 0.5).astype(int)
test_acc_single = np.mean(test_preds_single == y_test.values)

print("--- FINAL TEST SET EVALUATION ---")
print(f"Test Accuracy (CV Winner, λ=0.001):           {test_acc_cv * 100:.2f}%")
print(f"Test Accuracy (Single-Split Winner, λ=0):     {test_acc_single * 100:.2f}%")

--- FINAL TEST SET EVALUATION ---
Test Accuracy (CV Winner, λ=0.001):           77.46%
Test Accuracy (Single-Split Winner, λ=0):     77.63%
In [241]:
#Part h)
# i)

# =====================================================================
# Re-train all models and features from raw X values
# =====================================================================

# BASELINE 12 original features
X_train_base = X_train_raw.copy()
X_val_base = X_val_raw.copy()   # Added for val reporting
X_test_base = X_test_raw.copy()

spending_cols = ['RoomService', 'FoodCourt', 'ShoppingMall', 'Spa', 'VRDeck']
raw_spend_train = X_train_raw[spending_cols].sum(axis=1)
raw_spend_val = X_val_raw[spending_cols].sum(axis=1)   # Added for val reporting
raw_spend_test = X_test_raw[spending_cols].sum(axis=1)

# ENGINEERED (Add Log/NoSpend)
X_train_eng = X_train_base.copy()
X_val_eng = X_val_base.copy()   # Added for val reporting
X_test_eng = X_test_base.copy()

# Add NoSpending
X_train_eng['NoSpending'] = (raw_spend_train == 0).astype(int)
X_val_eng['NoSpending'] = (raw_spend_val == 0).astype(int)   # Added for val reporting
X_test_eng['NoSpending'] = (raw_spend_test == 0).astype(int)

# Add LogTotalSpending (Standardized using train stats!)
log_spend_train = np.log1p(raw_spend_train)
log_spend_val = np.log1p(raw_spend_val)   # Added for val reporting
log_spend_test = np.log1p(raw_spend_test)

train_mean_log = log_spend_train.mean()
train_std_log = log_spend_train.std()

X_train_eng['LogTotalSpending'] = (log_spend_train - train_mean_log) / train_std_log
X_val_eng['LogTotalSpending'] = (log_spend_val - train_mean_log) / train_std_log   # Added for val reporting
X_test_eng['LogTotalSpending'] = (log_spend_test - train_mean_log) / train_std_log

# POLYNOMIAL
X_train_poly = X_train_base.copy()
X_val_poly = X_val_base.copy()   # Added for val reporting
X_test_poly = X_test_base.copy()

# Add the Log and NoSpending features (same as above)
X_train_poly['NoSpending'] = X_train_eng['NoSpending']
X_val_poly['NoSpending'] = X_val_eng['NoSpending']   # Added for val reporting
X_test_poly['NoSpending'] = X_test_eng['NoSpending']

X_train_poly['LogTotalSpending'] = X_train_eng['LogTotalSpending']
X_val_poly['LogTotalSpending'] = X_val_eng['LogTotalSpending']   # Added for val reporting
X_test_poly['LogTotalSpending'] = X_test_eng['LogTotalSpending']

# Add 15 Polynomial Interactions
for col1, col2 in combinations_with_replacement(spending_cols, 2):
    new_col_name = f"{col1}_x_{col2}"
    
    raw_train_int = X_train_raw[col1] * X_train_raw[col2]
    raw_val_int = X_val_raw[col1] * X_val_raw[col2]   # Added for val reporting
    raw_test_int = X_test_raw[col1] * X_test_raw[col2]
    
    train_mean_int = raw_train_int.mean()
    train_std_int = raw_train_int.std()
    
    if train_std_int > 0:
        X_train_poly[new_col_name] = (raw_train_int - train_mean_int) / train_std_int
        X_val_poly[new_col_name] = (raw_val_int - train_mean_int) / train_std_int   # Added for val reporting
        X_test_poly[new_col_name] = (raw_test_int - train_mean_int) / train_std_int
    else:
        X_train_poly[new_col_name] = 0
        X_val_poly[new_col_name] = 0   # Added for val reporting
        X_test_poly[new_col_name] = 0

# Store the number of features for each model type
feature_counts = {
    '1. Baseline MSE': X_train_base.shape[1],
    '2. Baseline CE': X_train_base.shape[1],
    '3. Engineered CE': X_train_eng.shape[1],
    '4. Poly / Single-Split (λ=0)': X_train_poly.shape[1],
    '5. Best CV Winner (λ=0.001)': X_train_poly.shape[1]
}

train_results = {}
val_results = {}
test_results = {}

# 1. Baseline MSE
w_mse, _ = gradient_descent_mse(X_train_base.values, y_train.values, learning_rate=0.1, num_iterations=1000)
train_results['1. Baseline MSE'] = np.mean((np.dot(X_train_base.values, w_mse) >= 0.5).astype(int) == y_train.values)
val_results['1. Baseline MSE'] = np.mean((np.dot(X_val_base.values, w_mse) >= 0.5).astype(int) == y_val.values)
test_results['1. Baseline MSE'] = np.mean((np.dot(X_test_base.values, w_mse) >= 0.5).astype(int) == y_test.values)

# 2. Baseline CE
w_ce, _ = gradient_descent_ce(X_train_base.values, y_train.values, learning_rate=0.1, num_iterations=1000)
train_results['2. Baseline CE'] = np.mean((sigmoid(np.dot(X_train_base.values, w_ce)) >= 0.5).astype(int) == y_train.values)
val_results['2. Baseline CE'] = np.mean((sigmoid(np.dot(X_val_base.values, w_ce)) >= 0.5).astype(int) == y_val.values)
test_results['2. Baseline CE'] = np.mean((sigmoid(np.dot(X_test_base.values, w_ce)) >= 0.5).astype(int) == y_test.values)

# 3. Engineered CE
w_eng, _ = gradient_descent_ce(X_train_eng.values, y_train.values, learning_rate=0.1, num_iterations=1000)
train_results['3. Engineered CE'] = np.mean((sigmoid(np.dot(X_train_eng.values, w_eng)) >= 0.5).astype(int) == y_train.values)
val_results['3. Engineered CE'] = np.mean((sigmoid(np.dot(X_val_eng.values, w_eng)) >= 0.5).astype(int) == y_val.values)
test_results['3. Engineered CE'] = np.mean((sigmoid(np.dot(X_test_eng.values, w_eng)) >= 0.5).astype(int) == y_test.values)

# 4. Single-Split Winner / Polynomial CE (Lambda = 0)
w_single, _ = gradient_descent_ce_lambda(X_train_poly.values, y_train.values, l=0, learning_rate=0.1, num_iterations=1000)
train_results['4. Poly / Single-Split (λ=0)'] = np.mean((sigmoid(np.dot(X_train_poly.values, w_single)) >= 0.5).astype(int) == y_train.values)
val_results['4. Poly / Single-Split (λ=0)'] = np.mean((sigmoid(np.dot(X_val_poly.values, w_single)) >= 0.5).astype(int) == y_val.values)
test_results['4. Poly / Single-Split (λ=0)'] = np.mean((sigmoid(np.dot(X_test_poly.values, w_single)) >= 0.5).astype(int) == y_test.values)

# 5. Best CV Winner (Lambda = 0.001)
w_cv, _ = gradient_descent_ce_lambda(X_train_poly.values, y_train.values, l=0.001, learning_rate=0.1, num_iterations=1000)
train_results['5. Best CV Winner (λ=0.001)'] = np.mean((sigmoid(np.dot(X_train_poly.values, w_cv)) >= 0.5).astype(int) == y_train.values)
val_results['5. Best CV Winner (λ=0.001)'] = np.mean((sigmoid(np.dot(X_val_poly.values, w_cv)) >= 0.5).astype(int) == y_val.values)
test_results['5. Best CV Winner (λ=0.001)'] = np.mean((sigmoid(np.dot(X_test_poly.values, w_cv)) >= 0.5).astype(int) == y_test.values)

# --- PRINT THE UPDATED LEADERBOARD ---
print("-" * 88)
print(f"{'FINAL ACCURACY':<35} | {'Features':<9} | {'Train':<9} | {'Val':<9} | {'Test':<9}")
print("-" * 88)
for model in test_results.keys():
    f_count = feature_counts[model]
    t_acc = train_results[model] * 100
    v_acc = val_results[model] * 100
    te_acc = test_results[model] * 100
    print(f"{model:<35} | {f_count:>8} | {t_acc:>8.2f}% | {v_acc:>8.2f}% | {te_acc:>8.2f}%")
print("-" * 88)

----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
FINAL ACCURACY                      | Features  | Train     | Val       | Test     
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Baseline MSE                     |       12 |    76.59% |    75.10% |    77.52%
2. Baseline CE                      |       12 |    78.06% |    76.65% |    79.18%
3. Engineered CE                    |       14 |    75.24% |    73.66% |    76.37%
4. Poly / Single-Split (λ=0)        |       29 |    76.70% |    74.35% |    78.38%
5. Best CV Winner (λ=0.001)         |       29 |    76.68% |    73.95% |    78.26%
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

The trainning, validation, and test accuracies are all close to each other, which indicates the validation procedure was good. The best model was linear using Cross-Entropy loss

In [242]:
# ii)
feature_names = X_train_base.columns
abs_weights = np.abs(w_ce)

# argsort sorts ascending, so we take the last 3 [-3:] and reverse them [::-1]
top_3_indices = np.argsort(abs_weights)[-3:][::-1]

print("Top 3 Features with Largest Absolute Weights (CE Winner Model):")
print("-" * 65)
for i in top_3_indices:
    feature = feature_names[i]
    weight = w_ce[i]
    abs_w = abs_weights[i]
    print(f"{feature:<30} | Weight: {weight:>8.4f} | Absolute: {abs_w:>8.4f}")
print("-" * 65)

Top 3 Features with Largest Absolute Weights (CE Winner Model):
-----------------------------------------------------------------
Spa                            | Weight:  -7.8186 | Absolute:   7.8186
VRDeck                         | Weight:  -5.2066 | Absolute:   5.2066
RoomService                    | Weight:  -3.7047 | Absolute:   3.7047
-----------------------------------------------------------------

They are a little bit unexpected, as cryo sleep seemed very highly correlated with transportation. However, since the weights are negative, they make sense because not spending is heavily linked with transportation.

In [243]:
#Get indices for the features
idx_food = X_train_base.columns.get_loc('FoodCourt')
idx_spa = X_train_base.columns.get_loc('Spa')

#Extract weights
w0 = w_ce[0]          # Intercept
w_food = w_ce[idx_food] 
w_spa = w_ce[idx_spa]

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
plt.scatter(X_test['FoodCourt'], X_test['Spa'], c=y_test, 
            cmap='coolwarm', alpha=0.6, edgecolors='w', label='Test Samples')

# Calculate the Decision Boundary
x_vals = np.linspace(X_test['FoodCourt'].min(), X_test['FoodCourt'].max(), 100)
# Solve w0 + w_food*x + w_spa*y = 0  =>  y = -(w_food*x + w0) / w_spa
y_vals = -(w_food * x_vals + w0) / w_spa

plt.plot(x_vals, y_vals, '--k', linewidth=2, label='Decision Boundary')

plt.xlabel('FoodCourt Spending (Standardized)')
plt.ylabel('Spa Spending (Standardized)')
plt.title('Decision Boundary: FoodCourt vs Spa')
plt.legend()
plt.ylim(X_test['Spa'].min(), X_test['Spa'].max()) # Keep plot focused on data range
plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.show()

Visually, the separator does a good job keeping most red dots bellow and blue dots above

iv) Full table in part h) i). The loss function choice was the most important one across all models. Feature engineering made it slightly worse (although I expected it to make the models slightly better). Regularization mattered the least, which indicates that the model was most likely not overfitting (also indicated by close test/validation/trainning accuracies for all the models). Overall I would recommend cross entropy for someone building a classifier.
